coredata.dc:title: Promising photocatalytic degradation of lignin over carbon quantum dots decorated TiO2 nanocomposite in aqueous condition
coredata.dc:creator: Sharma, Shelja
Kumar, Sandeep
Arumugam, Senthil Murugan
Elumalai, Sasikumar
coredata.dc:description: The complex branched structure of lignin offers challenges in its chemical/biological transformation. The recalcitrant nature hampers its effective utilization towards the synthesis of potential chemicals and products through depolymerization. Herein, we demonstrate the effective process for lignin decomposition via photocatalysis using CQDs decorated TiO2 nanohybrid in aqueous conditions. Indeed, the sophisticated analytical determinations, including powder XRD, HR-TEM, and XPS, witnessed the structural characteristics of the as-prepared TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite. The photocatalyst promoted a nearly complete decomposition of commercial alkali lignin up to 99 % with the direct sunlight illumination, yielding a different variety of market-value lignin derivative products like m-anisic acid (3-methoxybenzoic acid) and p-hydroxybenzoic acid (PHBA) with 20 % and 16.25 % selectivity, respectively, through the probable oxidation mechanism. Upon verifying its effectiveness towards the decomposition of kraft lignin, it showed a promising response (80 % conversion) within 6 h of photocatalysis. The recyclability study showed that the photocatalyst has an exceptional photostability.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dot
Titanium dioxide
Lignin
Solar irradiation
Photocatalysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the as-prepared photocatalyst nanocomposite
Structural characterization of the as-prepared photocatalyst
Photocatalytic decomposition of lignin under solar light illumination using TiO2/CQDs
Mechanism of lignin photocatalytic degradation under sunlight using TiO2/CQDs and identification of intermediates

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Biotechnology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD pattern of as-prepared TiO2/CQDs composite nanomaterial.

(a) HR-TEM image of plain CQDs (b) lattice fringe of CQDs (Inset: particle size distribution of CQDs) (c) SAED of CQDs (d) typical lattice fringe of anatase phase TiO2 of nanocomposite (e) size and position of TiO2 and CQDs (f) SAED pattern of TiO2/CQDs.
(a) FE-SEM image of TiO2/CQDs (b) Elemental mapping of the TiO2/CQDs (c) Ti-KA, (d) O-KA, (e) C-KA (f) EDX spectrum of the nanocomposite.

(a) FTIR spectrum of TiO2, CQDs, and TiO2/CQDs (b) PL spectra of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs at the excitation wavelength (λ = 325 nm) (c) UV-DRS absorbance spectrum of TiO2/CQDs (d) Kubelka-Munk transformed reflectance spectrum of TiO2/CQDs.
(a) TGA spectrum of TiO2/CQDs (b) Raman spectrum of TiO2/CQDs (c) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm curve (d) Pore size distribution curve of TiO2/CQDs calculated from adsorption isotherm QSDFT.
XPS spectra of TiO2/CQDs (a) Survey (b) Ti 2p (c) C 1s and (d) O1s.

(a) Time-dependent UV–vis absorbance spectra of lignin illustrating solar light induced photocatalytic degradation of lignin (10 mg/L) using 0.5 g/L TiO2/CQDs (b) Assessment of photolysis (without catalyst), adsorption (without irradiation), and comparison of photocatalytic activity of TiO2/CQDs with TiO2-P25 (lignin concentration 10 mg/L, catalyst dose 0.5 g/L). (c) Influence of varied catalyst dose (TiO2/CQDs) on solar light-induced photocatalytic degradation of lignin (10 mg/L) (d) Influence of initial lignin concentration (10- 100 mg/L) on the photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2/CQDs (0.5 g/L).

(a) Graph of ln(C0/C) against time portraying photocatalytic degradation of lignin (10 mg/L lignin concentration, catalyst (TiO2/CQDs) dose 0.5 g/L), (b) UV–vis absorbance spectrum of photocatalytic degradation of agricultural biomass-derived kraft lignin (10 mg/L, pH 12) at different time intervals with TiO2/CQDs (1 g/L) under sunlight illumination. (c) Recyclability experiments of TiO2/CQDs photocatalyst for the lignin degradation (d) Fluorescence spectra of terephthalic acid in the presence of the catalyst under solar light irradiation depicting the production of hydroxyl radicals by catalyst at λex = 315 nm.
Diagrammatic illustration of charge separation and transfer in TiO2/CQDs.
Plausible oxidation pathway of degradation of lignin through photocatalysis.
FTIR spectra of lignin and sample obtained after photocatalytic reaction.

head.ce:title: Promising photocatalytic degradation of lignin over carbon quantum dots decorated TiO2 nanocomposite in aqueous condition

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemical Engineering Division, Center of Innovative and Applied Bioprocessing, Mohali 140306 India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Center of Innovative and Applied Bioprocessing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 1 Chemical Engineering Division, Center of Innovative and Applied Bioprocessing, Mohali 140306 India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dr. SSB University Institute of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014 India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. SSB University Institute of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 2Dr. SSB University Institute of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Panjab University, Chandigarh, 160014 India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The complex branched structure of lignin offers challenges in its chemical/biological transformation. The recalcitrant nature hampers its effective utilization towards the synthesis of potential chemicals and products through depolymerization. Herein, we demonstrate the effective process for lignin decomposition via photocatalysis using CQDs decorated TiO2 nanohybrid in aqueous conditions. Indeed, the sophisticated analytical determinations, including powder XRD, HR-TEM, and XPS, witnessed the structural characteristics of the as-prepared TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite. The photocatalyst promoted a nearly complete decomposition of commercial alkali lignin up to 99 % with the direct sunlight illumination, yielding a different variety of market-value lignin derivative products like m-anisic acid (3-methoxybenzoic acid) and p-hydroxybenzoic acid (PHBA) with 20 % and 16.25 % selectivity, respectively, through the probable oxidation mechanism. Upon verifying its effectiveness towards the decomposition of kraft lignin, it showed a promising response (80 % conversion) within 6 h of photocatalysis. The recyclability study showed that the photocatalyst has an exceptional photostability.

ce:section.ce:para: Lignin- one of the primary structural constituents of lignocellulosic biomass- is the most abundant renewable resource of aromatics in nature. In plants, it is mostly presented in the secondary cell wall section, and making up to 10–25 % of the plant biomass (dry basis). Typically, p-coumaryl alcohol, coniferyl alcohol, and sinapyl alcohol are the precursor that involves in the biosynthesis of lignin through the phenylpropanoid pathways, resulting in produces the complex lignin consisting of the corresponding monomeric units, such as p-hydroxyphenyl (H), guaiacyl (G), and syringyl (S), respectively [1]. Within lignin polymer, these units are cross-linked together through the carbon-carbon, ester, and ether linkages and thus provides complexity and high stability of the intramolecular bonds [2]. Lignin presents an extraordinary opportunity for being used in the production of bio-based products through efficient depolymerization techniques; for example, low molecular weight compounds, such as phenols, alkylphenols, and phenol resins, which has the potential to replace those obtained from fossil resources. However, it offers utilization challenges due to its heterogeneity [3,4]. Regardless of structural constraints (recalcitrant), the chemical catalytic transformation approach has come out as a fascinating scientific research objective for the lignin depolymerization. Till date, a variety of classical depolymerization protocols like thermochemical, oxidative, photocatalytic, biochemical, electrochemical, and/or hybrid depolymerization have been developed and employed to the native lignins; however, such protocols deliver a variety of end products under different operating conditions [5,6]. Among which, photocatalysis is being considered as an environment friendly and most appealing approach owing to its excellent benefits like clean, effective, energy-saving, and low cost [7]. Several routine studies have demonstrated that semiconductor photocatalysis, an integral part of advanced oxidation processes, is a prospective cutting-edge practice for the conversion of lignin to numerous valuable products, e.g., benzene, phenol, xylene, toluene and many more [6,8]. Photocatalysts are visible/solar active materials and are considered to be more competent than UV active photocatalysts [9]. Normally, sunlight accounts for only a 4–5 % fraction of UV light, and the rest of the spectrum falls in the visible/infra-red region. The catalysts fall under this category ensures a key to the challenges associated with the intermittent nature of sunlight and are also acknowledged as a renewable and absolute source of energy to stimulate activities on the earth.

To date, numerous semiconductor nanomaterials have been developed and tested as catalysts not only for the efficient removal of hazardous textile dyes, resistant pharmaceutical drugs, toxic heavy metal ions present in the aqueous medium but also for the photocatalytic reduction of carbon dioxide (CO2) and water (H2O) splitting processes [9,10]. Kobayakawa et al. is the one who first demonstrated the complete degradation of alkali lignin into CO2 and H2O molecules in the presence of TiO2 semiconductor materials under solar irradiation [11]. It is imperative to design the proficient solar/visible light active photocatalysts to achieve the higher photonic abilities and to reduce the consumption of energy needed for the irradiation. Among the popular semiconductor materials, TiO2 is trending in the photocatalytic applications due to its highly beneficial attitude. However, its high energy band gap value (3.2 eV) makes it less competitive due to the greater possibility that photoinduced electrons and holes could recombine, and radiation energy is lost or converted into heat when recombination takes place [12]. Sincere attempts are being made to enhance the activity of the photocatalyst by controlling the band gap, adjusting chemical composition, modifying the surface, and coupling or designing hybrid materials [13].

Literature studies reveal that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) greatly contributes in the improved photocatalytic activity of hybrid materials [14]. The characteristic CQDs tend to exhibit remarkable upconversion ability to convert light of longer wavelength to shorter wavelength for serving as a spectral converter for effective utilization of the full spectrum of sunlight [15]. Furthermore, the large electron storage capacity of CQDs facilitates the shuttling of photoexcited electrons from other semiconductors to its conducting network, thereby hindering the recombination of photogenerated carriers at the junction interface [16]. Therefore, the combination of CQDs and other metal oxide-based semiconductors could present an excellent strategy for the design of a high-performance photocatalyst for the enhanced catalytic activity [9]. Recently, Li et al. established its extended application for the photoreduction of 4-nitroaniline under visible light irradiation in the presence of a well-distributed CQDs generated via electrostatic interaction on branched polyethylenimine passivated TiO2 composites [17]. Wang et al. reported 3D CQDs/graphene aerogel composites via a facile hydrothermal method, which can efficiently improve the photosensitization efficiency of CQDs [18]. Likewise, Li et al. reported the effective degradation of lignins (both the mono- and dimeric model compounds and kraft pine lignin) using the as-prepared CdS/TiO2 as a visible light-responsive catalyst, yielding both the reactive phenolic and non-phenolic lignin as reaction products [19]. Theoretically, the presence of CQDs along with TiO2 could extend its absorption region to exploit the whole spectrum of freely available sunlight. Moreover, a stable photocatalyst is more appropriate to use in the degradation of recalcitrant lignin into its phenolic derivatives as an alternative yet valuable chemical source. With all these expectations, the present study evaluated the effectiveness of TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite prepared through the conventional hydrothermal method in the decomposition of alkali lignin towards the synthesis of simple aromatic molecules.

Analytical grade chemicals were purchased and used as received. Titanium isopropoxide (TTIP, >99.0 %), L-ascorbic acid (>99.0 %), NaOH (>99.0 %), terephthalic acid (>99.0 %), ethanol (C2H5OH, >99.0 %), TiO2P-25 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, India. Commercial alkali lignin (low sulfonate content) was procured from Sigma-Aldrich, India. Kraft lignin derived from agricultural biomass was received as a gift from CSIR-Central Food Technological Research Institute (CFTRI), Mysore, India. Deionized water (Merck Millipore system) was used to prepare all the stock solutions.

TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite was synthesized by the facile hydrothermal method. Briefly, TTIP (5 mL) was dispersed into 20 mL of ethanol and kept for continuous stirring for 30 min. Separately, an aqueous-ethanol mixture (25 mL) was prepared by dissolving equimolar ratio of water and ethanol. The latter was added dropwise into the TTIP solution under the constant stirring condition for 30 min, and immediately the whole reaction mixture turned turbid. The resultant turbid solution was then transferred to a Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. Before conducting the hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C, the autoclave was loaded with L-ascorbic acid solution for the synthesis of CQDs (preparation: 0.5 g L-ascorbic acid was dissolved into 25 mL of ethanol) and kept under continuous stirring for a few minutes. Upon completion of the reaction (4 h), the pale-yellow residues (precipitates) were obtained by high-speed centrifugation (4000 rpm) for 15 min. The collected precipitates were carefully washed with deionized water and ethanol until the pH of supernatant is neutral and then dried at 60 °C overnight. The obtained pale-yellow powder was calcined at 450 °C for 2 h. The optimum ratio of TiO2 and CQDs in the nanocomposite was 1:1. CQDs were prepared with the similar hydrothermal method without the precursors of TiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the as-prepared photocatalyst nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The crystal structure and phase characterization of the as-prepared photocatalysts were studied with powder X-ray diffraction (XRD; PANanalyticalXpert Pro.) technique using Cu-Kα (λ=1.54056 Å) and in the scanning angle range between 10° and 90°. The shape and size were determined by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM; JEOL-JEM-2100 Plus). The composition and mapping analyses were investigated by Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM; HITACHI, SU8010) and the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) technique. For the determination of structural changes of residual lignin, the post-reaction medium was firstly centrifuged to separate the solid catalysts, and the resultant catalyst-free solution was freeze-dried at −80 °C and lyophilized for 24 h. Further, it was dissolved in methanol. The band-gap of the nanocomposite was determined by UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrum (UV–vis DRS; Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer) using barium sulfate as a reference. The analysis of functional groups was measured on Fourier transform-infrared spectrometer (FTIR; Agilent Technologies Cary 600 series). The thermal stability analysis was performed on the thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA; Perkin Elmer STA 8000).

The surface area and pore distribution of the solid catalyst were computed by a multipoint Brunauer − Emmett − Teller (BET; Quantachrome Nova-2000e) method from the nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm at 77 K. The BET surface area was assessed within the range of relative pressures from 0.05 to 0.3. The total pore volume was calculated by measuring the amount of N2 adsorbed at a relative pressure (P/P0) of 0.99. The average pore width was calculated through the quenched state density functional theory (QSDFT) method using the embedded NovaWin software. The sample was degassed at 100 °C for 12 h before the analysis. The pH of the solution was adjusted using the online pH meter (Mettler-Toledo Five Easy Plus FP20) before introducing it for the analysis of the reaction products, including intermediates on the Liquid chromatography–mass spectroscopy instrument (LC–MS; Agilent Technologies 6120 Quadrupole LC/MS). The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum and UV–vis absorption spectra were collected on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi F-7000; 5J1−004 model) and double beam spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV 2600) instruments, respectively. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; PHI 5000 VersaProbe III spectrophotometer) instrument was used for studying the chemical state information from the surface of the catalyst being studied. The Raman spectrum was obtained from Raman spectrometer (WITEC alpha 300 R), which has a 600-line mm−1 with a 532 nm laser as an excitation source. The total organic content (TOC) was measured on TOC-L CPN Shimadzu analyzer.

Photocatalytic degradation of lignin was carried out in a cylindrical glass vessel containing 100 mL of lignin solution (10−100 mg/L) with varied dose levels (0.1−0.75 g/L) of the as-prepared photocatalyst (TiO2/CQDs). Initially, the reaction mixture was kept under constant stirring for 30 min under the dark for the attainment of adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The cylindrical photoreactor vessel was then illuminated with direct solar light of the average intensity of 70 Klux. Aliquots of 1 mL were withdrawn after every 1 h, and were made to pass through the 0.22 μm PTFE filter. The collected supernatant was analyzed on a double beam UV–vis spectrophotometer to record the absorption maxima of lignin as a function of time. The change in concentration of lignin (C/C0) was acquired from the absorbance calibration curve (A/A0), according to the Beer-Lambert’s law, where C0 is the initial concentration, and A0 is the corresponding value for absorbance. The photocatalytic degradation of lignin was studied with respect to the changes in absorption spectra and reduction in TOC value. The percent degradation of lignin was computed from the equation (Eq. 1) as follows:
(1)
%
l i g n i n
d e g r a d a t i o n =
C 0
- C
C 0
× 100
where, C0 represents the initial concentration of lignin before sunlight irradiation, and C represents the concentrations of lignin at a time, t. All the experiments were carried out in duplicate, and the error bars are plotted in the results.

Similarly, for evaluating the catalyst recycle performance, the post-reaction medium carefully collected and catalyst was washed thoroughly using deionized water to remove the surface adsorbed lignin and dried well. The production of hydroxyl (
OH) radical species in the photocatalytic reaction was monitored by PL technique. Briefly, 0.5 mM terephthalic acid and 2 mM NaOH were dissolved together in 100 mL of deionized water. Photocatalyst (TiO2/CQDs) of a certain dose was dispersed into the prepared solution. Prior to the sunlight illumination and addition of the catalyst, the sample was withdrawn from the resultant solution as blank for comparison. Similarly, samples were collected intermittently during the photocatalytic reaction and were analyzed by the PL spectroscopy technique at λex = 315 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of the as-prepared photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para:
The collective studies often highlighted that photocatalytic performance of the catalyst mainly depends upon the crystallinity, light absorption, and recombination of photoinduced charge carriers characteristics [17–19]. A variety of spectroscopic and analytical techniques were employed for the detailed characterization of the as-prepared nanocomposite (TiO2/CQDs) to determine its suitability in the lignin decomposition reaction under aqueous conditions. Powder XRD is a rapid analytical technique used for the phase identification of any crystalline material that can provide the information on unit cell dimensions. The analysis revealed that the nanocomposite was crystalline (as shown in Fig. 1 ). For instance, the notable diffraction peaks at 2θ= 25.52°, 38.04°, 48.18°, 54.22°, 55.19°, 63.02°, 68.95°, and 70.55° were assigned to the anatase crystal phase of TiO2 (JCPDS card no. 21-1272) [20]. Furthermore, a diffraction peak at 2θ=31° analogous to (211) plane of brookite phase (as * marked in Fig. 1) was also observed (JCPDS card no. 29-1360) [21]. Several apparent studies established that the formation of the brookite phase could take place in a hydrothermal synthesis approach often as a by-product while precipitation [22]. On a lighter note, the diffraction peak in the range from 2θ = 18°-22°, typically corresponds to CQDs, was absent due to the minute content of CQDs in the nanocomposite. But, it could also be assumed that the diffraction intensity of CQDs was relatively lower as compared to TiO2 [23]. Similarly, the observed diffraction plane (002) corresponding to 2θ= 22.5° depicted the presence of graphitic carbon [12], as shown in Fig. S1. Further, the absence of any other diffraction peaks in the XRD spectrum indicates that the as-prepared nanocomposite is highly pure. Typical Debye-Scherrer’s equation (Eq. 2) was used for the estimation of crystallite size (Dhkl ) of the nanocomposite, as stated below:\
(2)
D
h k l
=
K λ
β
h k l
C o s θ
where, K is the numerical factor, often denoted as crystallite shape factor; λ is the incident wavelength (1.54056 Å); βhkl is the full width at half maxima value of the diffraction peak (in radians); θ is the Bragg’s angle, and h, k, l are the Miller indices of the planes being analyzed. Upon assigning all the values in Eq. 2, the D hkl, was computed to be around 23.13 nm from the (101) diffraction plane. For tetragonal crystal system, following equation (Eq. 3) is used for the determination of lattice parameters “a” and “c”, presented as:
(3)
1
d 2
=
h 2
+
k 2
a 2
+
l 2
c 2
where, d is the interplanar spacing, and a and c are the unit cell dimensions or lattice parameters. From the tetragonal crystal system equation, the values of the lattice parameters a and c were measured to be 3.7775 Å and 9.46228 Å, respectively. The deviation from the actual values of the lattice constants to the tetragonal anatase unit cell, i.e., a =3.7821 Å and c =9.5022 Å could be due to the incorporation of CQDs into the TiO2 matrix.

The surface morphologies of the plain CQDs and the nanocomposite TiO2/CQDs were characterized using the HR-TEM. The images reveal that CQDs are quasi-spherical in shape, and the average diameter was less than 1 nm, as shown in Fig. 2 a [24]. Furthermore, it exhibited the narrow size distribution arrangement of CQDs, ranging from 0.10 nm to 0.70 nm with average diameter of 0.40 nm, as determined by statistical analysis of hundred particles by using the ImageJ software, as displayed in Fig. 2b (inset). Likewise, Fig. 2b exposed that the crystal lattice spacing around 0.32 nm, which agreed well with the (002) spacing of graphitic carbon (JCPDS card no. 46-0943) [25]. It is also confirmed from the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the CQDs, as shown in Fig. 2c [26]. Fig. 2d-e display the HRTEM image of the nanocomposite TiO2/CQDs and it can be noticed that the characteristic lattice fringe of nearly spherical anatase phase of TiO2 with interplanar spacing 3.48 Å corresponds to (101) diffraction plane [27]. Also, the size of the nanocomposite measured was around 2−3 nm in average diameters (Fig. 2e), sizes bigger than plain CQDs, clearly due to the deposition of CQDs on the surface of TiO2. The appearance of the distinct spots in the HR-TEM images corresponded to the presence of TiO2 (light grey spots) and CQDs (dark spots) as CQDs were bound closely to the surface of TiO2, since virtually no free CQDs was observed [23]. However, there is a lot of chance that the composite nanoparticles could be formed by the sporadic deposition of CQDs on the surface of TiO2, which has the potential to prevent the TiO2 nanoparticles from aggregating [28]. Furthermore, the well-separated diffraction spots in the corresponding SAED pattern, as shown in Fig. 2f, which displays the distinct diffraction planes, i.e., (101), (004), (200) and (204) of TiO2 [29,30]. Overall, the findings from TEM supported well enough the characteristics, particularly the presence of CQDs in TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite, and also, found consistent with XRD results.

The morphology and surface characteristics of the nanocomposite were investigated by FE-SEM technique. It can be noticed from Fig. 3 that nanoparticles were spherical in shape, and the presence of CQDs did not significantly influence the shape, size, and spherical morphologies of TiO2 nanoparticles. Furthermore, the nanoparticles were highly dense and possessed a grain-like structure with diameters less than 5 nm, which is well supported by HR-TEM analysis result. Similarly, the EDX mapping images exhibit the varied distribution of all the elements in a nanocomposite. However, the percentage of carbon was not as high as that of other elements, such as titanium and oxygen due to the fact that the loading of carbon in the form of CQDs was relatively less. No other element could be seen in the EDX mapping (as data provided in Table S1), indicating that the as-prepared nanocomposite is highly pure.

The typical CQDs are composed of carbon, hydrogen and oxygen, and its elemental ratio primarily depends on the employed synthesis methodology and choice of precursors. Furthermore, the surface of C-dots is quite prone to organic or inorganic moieties, and the surface passivation and modification are the key factors in order to form a highly fluorescent CQDs. To date, wide studies have employed the hydrothermal method for the synthesis of CQDs due to its attractive features like inexpensive and eco-friendly to synthesize the CQDs from saccharides, amines, organic acids and their derivatives. Subsequently, those studies preferred to acquire the FTIR spectra to determine its functional groups [31–33]. Based on the literature reports, the acquired FTIR spectrum of CQDs, TiO2, and TiO2/CQDs established a broad characteristic band at 3258 cm−1 ascribed to the water adsorbed onto the surface in the form of hydroxyl (OH−) anions [31]. While comparing results with the other samples, CQDs exhibited more a pronounced band at 3258 cm−1 (as shown in Fig. 4 a) likely due to the existence of large numbers of residual hydroxyl groups in the plain CQDs [32]. The minor yet sharp peak at 1638 cm−1 corresponds to the existence of the carbonyl group in CQDs [33]. Similarly, the distinct peaks at 1370 cm−1and 1240 cm-1 correspond to the symmetrical and asymmetrical stretching vibration bands of COC (epoxy) and CO bonds, respectively, which could also be observed in the target TiO2/CQDs sample. However, no such peak was observed in the plain TiO2 sample [31]. Thus, the results suggest that the surface of CQDs is functionalized with epoxy, carbonyl, hydroxyl and carboxyl acid moieties. Furthermore, the characteristic peak of TiO2 due to stretching band vibrations of Ti-O bond could be observed in the region 400−600 cm−1. The analysis results also suggest that the oxygen in CQDs might bind to oxygen-deficient positions of TiO2, and the nanohybrid is constructed via covalent bonding between CQDs and TiO2 through oxygen [32,33]. Generally, CQDs show weight loss to some extent during the calcination at 450 °C [34], however, that doesn’t signify the complete removal of CQDs from the nanocomposite. The outcomes from FTIR and other analytical techniques supported the presence of CQDs in the nanocomposite.

The optical characteristics, the band gap values, and the types of electronic transitions of TiO2/CQDs were examined in detail by UV–vis DRS technique. The PL emission spectrum is beneficial for understanding the behaviour of the photoinduced charge carriers and their corresponding recombination probability. The emission generally arises from the signals that are instigated by the recombination of the photo-generated charge carriers. Thus, it could be assumed that the lower PL emission intensity is directly related to improved photocatalytic activity [13]. Room-temperature PL spectra of TiO2 and TiO2/CQDs were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 325 nm (Fig. 4b). It can be noticed that all the peaks possessed a similar shape line.

The noticeable sharp and distinctive peak at 365 nm in both cases is attributed to the recombination of photo-generated charge transporters; however, the lesser intensity was observed in the case of TiO2/CQDs as compared to the pristine anatase, possibly due to the proficient transfer of electrons from the conduction band of TiO2 to CQDs [12,35]. The bonding of Ti-O-C could then promote faster electron transfer in the interfacial region of the TiO2/CQDs composite structure, and thus, hindering carrier recombination [36]. Therefore, it could be concluded that the radical trapping and recombination of electrons and holes obstructed intensely in TiO2/CQDs than the plain TiO2. The trivial peaks at around 471 nm and 535 nm are ascribed to the blue-green emission band, metal atom defects and crystal defects, or oxygen vacancies [37].

Since anatase TiO2 was used to demonstrate an absorption threshold around or lesser than 400 nm, mostly in the ultraviolet region, the absorption edge of TiO2/CQDs was shifted towards the visible region (Fig. 4c), implying more absorption capability for visible light could be predicted. This subsequently enhanced light absorption towards the visible region is attributed to the chemical interactions of Ti–O–C bonds between TiO2 and CQDs [38]. The possible interactions in the nanocomposite could effectively modify the rate of interfacial transport of carriers, which is quite beneficial to the photocatalysis process under solar light illumination. The energy band gap (Eg ) was calculated from the Tauc’s plot (Fig. 4d) by applying Kubelka- Munk rule between (αhυ)n and photon energy (hυ) using the relation (Eq. 4) as:
(4)
( α h υ )
n
= A
× ( h υ -
E g
)
where, A and α are the constant and absorption coefficient, respectively. For the anatase phase of TiO2, being an indirect band gap semiconductor, the value of n is 0.5, and Eg is reported to be 3.20 eV. However, with the presence of CQDs along with TiO2, the value of the energy band gap got reduced to 2.88 eV. The decrease in Eg value from 3.20 eV to 2.88 eV suggested that nanocomposite could possibly harness the light of all wavelengths and possess enhanced photocatalytic activity than plain TiO2 [39]. CQDs are also known to display fascinating and unique upconversion PL properties. Typically, the upconverted materials could transfer low energy photons to high energy photons by absorbing two or multiple longer wavelength photons, resulting in enhanced visible light response and utilization. Therefore, it can be reasonably expected that CQDs could act as an electron mediator, photosensitizer, and a spectral converter in the photocatalytic processes. Thus, typical CQDs serve as a versatile material and has the prospect in the designing of a high-performance photocatalyst [13,39].

Conventional TGA measures the weight changes in a nanomaterial as a function of temperature or time under a controlled environment, and it is generally used to examine the thermal stability of the catalysts. It could be seen from Fig. 5 a that the measurement was done for the catalyst for two weight loss regions, i.e., up to temperature 250 °C, where an insignificant weight loss (1.13 %) was achieved, which corresponded to the loss of adsorbed water molecules over the surface of the catalyst. The other region was between 250 °C and 1000 °C, where a weight loss of around 7.6 % was noticed, which is related to the dissipation of functional groups containing oxygen moieties (such as epoxy, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) attached to the surface of CQDs via covalent linkages [39]. Overall, the TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite had a total weight loss of 8.73 % throughout the temperature measurements, indicating that it has a higher thermal stability property.

Raman spectroscopy, a widely established analytical technique employed for molecular morphology and characterization of carbon materials, was used to reveal the extraordinary structure and unusual electronic properties of the as-prepared nanocomposite (TiO2/CQDs). The acquired spectra revealed that four distinctive peaks centered at 149, 402, 518, and 641 cm−1 and a weak peak at 201 cm−1 (Fig. 5b) could be associated to Eg(1), B1g, A1g and Eg(3), and Eg(2), corresponding to Raman active modes of pure anatase TiO2 and found consistent with the literature reports [40]. Furthermore, the two distinguished peaks at 1365 and 1612 cm–1 could be attributed to the defect band (D band) and graphite band (G band) [12,40]. The G band is related to the vibrations of carbon atoms (sp2‐bonded) in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice. Contrarily, the D band is ascribed to the defects and disorders in the structure of carbon materials. Thus, the analysis result witnessed that CQDs were successfully anchored on TiO2; however, more defects could be suspected in the structure of TiO2/CQDs due to the existence of oxygen vacancies in CQDs.

The specific surface area of the solid catalyst plays an essential role in explaining the enhanced photocatalytic activity. Catalysts with the large specific surface area are supposed to exhibit better photocatalyst performance because they provide a large number of active sites for the efficient adsorption of the reactant compounds. The specific surface area, pore size, pore-volume, and other textural properties and porosity of TiO2/CQDs were studied by the BET method. Fig. 5c presents the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm curve of TiO2/CQDs and Fig. 5d shows the pore size distribution of the nanohybrid computed from the adsorption curve of the isotherm using the QSDFT method. It is evident that the synthesized nanocomposite followed a type IV isotherm of mesoporous nanomaterials. The specific surface area and pore size distribution of TiO2/CQDs were estimated to be 100.86 m2/g and 34.9 nm, respectively. Thus, it can be expected that such a high specific surface area of the catalyst assures the proficient adsorption of lignin molecules on to the surface of the catalyst is considered to be the primary and essential step for initiating the photocatalytic reaction. Furthermore, the BET results suggested that the mesoporous nanomaterials with a narrow pore size distribution could deliver a better photocatalytic activity owing to the presence of more active sites over the catalyst surface.

The chemical composition of TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite was inspected using XPS. The full-scale/survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 6 a) and high-resolution images approve the presence of titanium (Ti), oxygen (O), and carbon (C), respectively. In the deconvoluted spectra of Ti, two peaks were observed at 458.7 eV and 464.5 eV, corresponding to Ti 2p 3/2 and Ti 2p ½, respectively (Fig. 6b) [41]. Furthermore, in the deconvoluted spectra of C 1s, three peaks located at 284.8 eV, 286.0 eV, and 288.5 eV were witnessed, attributing to CC (graphite), CO and O=C-O bonds, respectively (Fig. 6c) [42]. These peaks confirm the presence of CQDs in the nanocomposite, and the findings are also found consistent with the HRTEM, Raman, EDX, and FTIR results. In the deconvoluted O 1s spectrum (Fig. 6d), the observed peaks at 529.8 eV, 530.6 eV, and 531.9 eV were accredited to the presence of Ti − O, OH, and O − C bonds, respectively, also confirming the existence of the CQDs [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic decomposition of lignin under solar light illumination using TiO2/CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic activity of the nanocomposite was evaluated under direct sunlight illumination in aqueous medium using lignin (commercial lignin) as the model compound. Operational parameters, such as catalyst dose, initial lignin concentration, and time of the photocatalytic reaction, were optimized to obtain the optimum conditions for lignin degradation. Lignin exhibited maximum absorbance at λmax=279 nm, which could be ascribed to the n-ᴫ* electronic transitions of unconjugated phenolic hydroxyl groups or etherified hydroxyl groups and aromatic groups in lignin units and another absorbance peak at 201 nm was due to the ᴫ-ᴫ* electronic transitions of unsaturated aromatics [44]. The UV–vis absorbance spectra (Fig. 7 a) represents that the absorbance intensity of lignin at λmax was gradually decreasing in a time-dependent manner. Fascinatingly, the reaction achieved the highest lignin (initial conditions: 10 mg/L and pH 12) degradation capacity of 99.24 % after 4 h with 0.5 g/L dose of TiO2/CQDs under direct sunlight irradiation without the supplementation of any oxidizing agents. In a similar manner, a separate experiment was conducted in the dark without sunlight to investigate the role of adsorption in the reaction, resulting in about a 29 % decrease in absorbance intensity after 1 h, attributed to the adsorption of lignin molecules over the surface of the catalyst. It was also noticed that complete adsorption (up to 23 %) was achieved once the catalyst attains the adsorption-desorption equilibrium after 4 h (Fig. 7b). Thus, the findings implied that sunlight illumination (light source) played a key role in the photocatalytic reaction, and also, TiO2/CQDs is active under solar/visible light. In order to validate the catalyst’s potential, the photolysis reaction was performed without the catalyst under similar conditions, which resulted in no significant degradation (merely 3.8 %), suggesting that only photolysis proved to be incompetent for the lignin degradation. The results show that the combined effect of sunlight and photocatalyst (TiO2/CQDs) complemented the photocatalytic reaction and had a significant impact in the complete degradation of lignin (Fig. 7b). As a result, highest lignin degradation of 99.24 % was achieved within 4 h of sunlight illumination. For comparison, the photocatalytic reaction using a commercial photocatalyst TiO2-P25 under similar conditions (10 mg/L lignin concentration, 0.5 g/L catalyst dose, and pH 12) was performed, which resulted in a 65.34 % lignin decomposition (Fig. 7b). The comparative results inferred that the former is promising in the lignin photocatalytic degradation.

To investigate the effect of the CQDs loading on the photocatalytic degradation of lignin, the nanocomposite with different ratios of TiO2 to CQDs were prepared viz. 1:0.5, 1:1 and 1:1.5. An increasing trend of lignin decomposition was achieved with the incremented CQDs loading on to the TiO2 up to 1:1 ratio, likely due to the enhanced absorbance for solar light and improved separation efficiency of photoinduced charge carriers. Further increase of CQDs content to 1:1.5 ratio led to a decreased photocatalytic performance, could be due to the competition for visible light absorption. Presumably, the excess CQDs tend to accumulate on the surface and pores of TiO2, which may obstruct the light harvesting, thereby leading to the decreased separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers. Overall, the results show that 1:1 combination of TiO2 to CQDs exhibited a better result say a near complete decomposition of lignin (99.2 %) than the other combinations, i.e., 1.1–1.41 times relatively higher. Further comparing the result with the yield of commercial TiO2-P25 catalyzed decomposition reaction, it is approximately 1.52 times higher. Therefore, the optimum ratio of TiO2 to CQDs maintained for further photocatalytic reactions was 1:1.

The influence of catalyst (TiO2/CQDs) dose was studied to identify the minimal and optimum catalyst dose for the photocatalytic degradation of lignin. The study was conducted at varying concentrations ranging from 0.25 g/L to 0.75 g/L, while keeping all other experimental parameters unchanged (10 mg/L lignin conc. and 4 h). As the catalyst dose was increased from 0.25 g/L to 0.35 g/L, the percentage degradation was also found to be consistently increasing from 65.2 %–83.24 % (Fig. 7c), probably be attributed to the boost in the number of active sites present on the surface of the photocatalyst that rises with the catalyst dosage. Further increase in dosage level up to 0.5 g/L achieved a nearly complete lignin degradation due to the tendency of the substrate that occupied the maximum number of active sites of the catalyst since catalyst loading is completely dependent on initial solute concentration. Beyond the optimum loading, the catalyst showed a detrimental effect in the lignin decomposition, i.e., degradation efficiency was dropped to 84.73 % at 0.75 g/L catalyst dose. It might be caused due to the increased turbidity of the solution with an increased catalyst dose that typically hampers the effective penetration of photons/sunlight, resulting in decreases in the photoactive volume of the suspension [45]. Thus, the findings advised that the mere increase in catalyst does not promise a higher degradation rate. Also, it may not be beneficial for both aggregation and diminished radiation owing to the light scattering phenomenon [9,13].

The impact of initial lignin concentration on the degradation efficiency of lignin under sunlight using TiO2/CQDs was studied by varying the initial reactant concentrations like 10, 25, 50 and 100 mg/L, while maintaining all other reaction parameters constant (catalyst dose 0.5 g/L and natural pH 12). It was observed that the extent of photodegradation decreased with the corresponding rise in the initial lignin concentrations, arranged in the order as 99.24 % > 95 % > 86.39 % > 62.65 %, respectively (Fig. 7d). Thus, the response helps in a better understanding of the absorption phenomena of the catalyst that as lignin concentration increases, the percentage degradation decreases due to the reduced availability of active sites for the higher lignin concentrations. Indeed, photocatalysis highly relies on the initial concentration of the substrate, and lower substrate concentrations could yield potential degradation products [34,45]. Also, comparative photocatalytic experiments were conducted under different light conditions like blue (450−490 nm), green (520−560 nm), and yellow (560−590 nm) using the light-emitting diodes (LED). It is known that LED light can generate a narrow spectrum that can also be adjusted through its design. Consequently, a varying decomposition percentages with respect to the light conditions, i.e., 58.78 %, 78.43 %, and 61.18 % for the blue, green, and yellow light, respectively (Fig. S3) were achieved. While comparison, a relatively 20–40 % lower degradation results were produced than the direct sunlight illumination.

The FE-SEM images of the substrate (commercial lignin) before and after the photocatalysis process at the optimized conditions were shown to present the structural changes that occurred during the photocatalytic reaction. The evident images show that the surface of lignin before reaction seemed to be smooth, along with big clusters (Fig. S2a), while a surface distortion was noticed at the same magnification with the lignin after the photocatalytic reaction (up to 4 h), suggesting lignin underwent a significant structural change (Fig. S2b). The close analysis reveals that a substantial rupture and a lot of cracks have emerged on the surface during the decomposition reaction that led to assume that fewer weak bonds would be broken, leading to the degradation of lignin and the formation of monomers.

The total organic carbon (TOC) content in water is an important parameter to assess the concentration of any known single organic compound in the aqueous system; it also provides a method of analyzing the degree of contamination in wastewater and the quality of water. It has been reported that varieties of organic compounds present in aqueous solutions could undergo the oxidation to CO2 with TiO2 powder and UV illumination via photocatalysis [46]. For verifying the attitude, TOC analysis was performed for the lignin before and after the photocatalytic degradation. The results show that the value of the lignin sample before initiating the photocatalytic reaction (before irradiation) was determined to be 50.22 mg/L, and it got gradually decreased to 10.11 mg/L at the end of the photocatalytic reaction (TiO2/CQDs with sunlight irradiation). Based on the outcomes, the lignin solution was not only decolorized but also underwent mineralization process, i.e., a decrease in the TOC data reveals that 79.8 % degree of mineralization of lignin was achieved after the photocatalytic treatment. In accordance with the literature reports, most lignin photocatalytic degradation reactions have been conducted under UV light source at a higher dose of catalyst (1−10 g/L) [47] and prolonged-time intervals up to 20 h [48]. Herein, CQDs modified TiO2 (0.5 g/L) promoted a maximum photocatalytic efficiency (99.24 %) for the decomposition of lignin (10 mg/L) within 4 h of sunlight illumination.

The photocatalytic degradation/oxidation kinetics of several organic compounds have usually been modelled using the Langmuir– Hinshelwood (L-H) equation since it considers the adsorption characteristics of the substrate on the surface of the catalyst. The popular L-H expression explains the kinetics of heterogeneous photocatalytic systems that can be reduced to the first-order kinetic equation (Eq. 5), as:
L i g n i n
⟶ k
D e g r a d i t i o n
p r o d u c t
(5)
l n
C 0
C
= k t
where, C0 and C are the initial and final concentrations of lignin (mg/mL); k is the apparent rate constant (min−1), and t is the time (min) of the photocatalytic reaction. The photocatalytic degradation of lignin under optimized conditions confirmed the pseudo-first-order kinetics with rate constant, k of 0.00772 min−1, based on the ln(C0/C) vs t plot, as shown in Fig. 8 a.

Further, the effectiveness of TiO2/CQDs on photodegradation of biomass-derived kraft lignin was verified under the optimal conditions. For confirming its structural characteristics, the UV–vis absorbance was recorded, which revealed that kraft lignin has a similar fashion of absorption spectrum as that of commercial alkali lignin (Fig. 8b). Upon subjecting it to the photocatalytic reaction, nearly 55 % lignin degradation was achieved within 1 h TiO2/CQDs (1 g/L conc.), attributed to the synergistic effect of surface adsorption and photocatalysis. The extended reaction, i.e., up to 6 h, resulted in maximum lignin degradation of 80 %, as presented in Fig. 8b. Thus, the results established that TiO2/CQDs nanocomposite is a versatile photocatalyst material that could assist in the decomposition of the variety of bioderived lignins under the solar light condition with a minimal catalyst dosage.

The stability of the catalyst is a critical issue in the typical heterogeneous catalysis. A reusability study was performed to estimate the stability of the catalyst with similar experimental conditions of lignin photodegradation. The photocatalytic efficiency of the catalyst for three consecutive cycles was calculated to be 99 %, 95 %, and 92 %, implying that the photocatalyst has an exceptional photostability during lignin degradation (Fig. 8c).

The generation of reactive oxidizing species in the photocatalytic system, i.e., hydroxyl radicals, responsible for the potential degradation of lignin was investigated by terephthalic acid PL technique. It is believed that terephthalic acid is non-fluorescent in nature; however, upon reacting with
OH radicals, terephthalic acid got converted to 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid, which is highly fluorescent in nature. It could be observed from Fig. 8d that the intensity of the emission peak was gradually increasing with the increase in the length of reaction at the emission wavelength of 415 nm, implying that a large number of
OH radicals were generating in the process, which is converting the terephthalic acid to 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of lignin photocatalytic degradation under sunlight using TiO2/CQDs and identification of intermediates
ce:section.ce:para: The plausible reaction mechanism for the explicit understanding of photocatalytic degradation of lignin under direct sunlight has been proposed in Fig. 9 . In definition, when the TiO2 is illuminated by solar light, the electrons in the valence band of TiO2 got excited and moved to the conduction band of TiO2. However, due to the large band gap (3.2 eV), electrons used to move back towards the valence band, and these photogenerated charge carriers could recombine, resulting in loss of energy and brought insignificant degradation of the organic compound. Alternatively, the incorporation of CQDs in the system could change the present scenario completely.

Due to a low energy band gap (2.88 eV) of the nanohybrid, the electrons reached the conduction band instead of electrons moving back to the valence band and further progressed freely to CQDs. The whole process obstructed the recombination of electrons and holes, and led to the efficient separation of photoinduced charge carriers (electrons and holes), thereby enhancing the degradation rate. Thus, the holes in the valence band reacted with adsorbed water molecules to generate the hydroxyl (
OH) radicals. Also, the electrons transferred from the conduction band of TiO2 to the excited state of CQDs and reacted with adsorbed oxygen, thereby contributing significantly to the production of superoxide radicals. Alongside, the more extended wavelength irradiation in sunlight was absorbed by the CQDs to generate photoinduced charge carriers that could be recombined, and the light of shorter wavelength was emitted due to the upconversion characteristics of CQDs, which could be effectively absorbed by TiO2. The superoxide radicals yielded a great amount of
OH radical species in the photocatalytic system upon protonation, which held accountable for the efficient degradation of lignin. The above hypothesis is also evidenced by the terephthalic acid PL approach. The upconversion phenomenon of CQDs could greatly surge the utilization capacity of solar energy and, thus, ultimately extend the photo-response range. The improved photocatalytic efficiency of TiO2/CQDs is vividly accredited to the charge transport between TiO2 and CQDs, representing improved efficiency of the composite, and is also suggested by PL and UV-DRS spectroscopy. In a nutshell, obstructed recombination, efficient charge transfer, and upconversion phenomenon of CQDs aligned for the enhanced lignin degradation in the aqueous phase.

The plausible degradation products of lignin via the oxidation process (Fig. 10 ) was determined with the help of LCMS technique. The lignin sample showed a major peak at m/z = 475 before the photocatalytic reaction. From the Fig. 10, it is apparent that the whole R group moiety got oxidized to benzoic acid group, while the hydroxy and methoxy groups at p- and m-positions, respectively, remain intact in the lignin structure. Hence, 3,4-dihydroxy-5-methoxybenzoic acid (m/z = 185, structure 1) could be probably formed after the oxidation with 41.4 % selectivity and 69.4 % conversion of lignin compound. The consequences of loss of hydroxy and methoxy groups from different positions of lignin, resulted in 3-methoxybenzoic acid (commonly referred as m-anisic acid) and 4-hydroxybenzoic acid or p-hydroxybenzoic acid (PHBA) (structures 2 and 3), based on the m/z values of 175 (sodium adduct) and 157 (water adduct) after 2 and 4 h of the degradation reaction, respectively. Approximately, 20 % and 16 % selectivities were achieved for the respective compounds with 99.2 % conversion. Pyrocatechol (structure 5) with m/z value of 129 (water adduct) could be likely formed from 3,4-dihydroxy-5-methoxybenzoic acid (structure 1) through the decarboxylation of 3,4-dihydroxybenzoic acid after 2 h of the photocatalytic degradation of lignin (Fig. S4) with 35 % selectivity. Therefore, it could be summarized that lignin underwent degradation process since all the mass fragments have m/z values less than 200.

The characteristic structure of lignin material before and after degradation was verified with FTIR spectrum through the distinction of the structures of syringyl (S), guaiacyl (G) and p-hydroxyphenyl (H) propane units, as shown in Fig. 11 . For instance, the recorded peaks at 1592 cm−1, 1506 cm−1, and 1420 cm−1 are the characteristic peaks of H unit, 1323, 1217 and 1130 cm−1 belong to the S unit, whereas 1035 and 926 cm−1 belong to the G unit [50]. Thus, the FTIR spectrum witnessed that the polymer lignin is composed of H, S, and G interlinked units. The structure of the H propane unit was found to be diminished in the lignin molecule after 4 h of photocatalytic treatment, as compared to the substrate lignin [51]. An additional peak at 1657 cm−1 was observed for the reaction solution after the photocatalysis process, which is ascribed to the −CO stretching frequency of the carboxylic acid group. The chromatographic analysis established that all the possible reaction intermediates or degraded products were having a carboxylic acid group after the oxidation. The FTIR outcomes confirm the degradation of polymeric lignin into the simpler molecules and are also actively supporting the proposed pathway through the results of liquid chromatography.

In summary, CQDs decorated TiO2 nanocomposite was synthesized through a facile hydrothermal technique and subsequently verified its structural properties, including structure, morphology, and optical properties using the high-end analytical and spectroscopic instrumentation techniques. The as-prepared TiO2/CQDs catalyst showed enhanced photocatalytic activity in the degradation of alkali lignin (up to 99 %) under solar light illumination. Whereas, the TiO2 –P25 (model commercial photocatalyst) could achieve only 65 % degradation under similar reaction conditions. The reduction in the TOC value before and after the photocatalytic reaction reveals that a 79.8 % degree of mineralization of lignin was achieved. The photocatalytic decomposition of lignin obeyed the pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics, according to the L-H adsorption model. Upon validation of the catalyst's effectiveness, it was able to decompose the biomass-derived kraft lignin up to 80 % within 6 h of sunlight irradiation, expressing that TiO2/CQDs is versatile. The superlative photocatalytic activity is attributed to the presence of CQDs in the nanocomposite that obstructed both the recombination and quick transfer of photo-generated charge carriers so as to harness the entire spectrum of solar light efficiently.

Shelja Sharma: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Sandeep Kumar: Visualization, Investigation. Senthil Murugan Arumugam: Software, Formal analysis, Resources. Sasikumar Elumalai: Conceptualization, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Department of Biotechnology (DBT), New Delhi, India for their consistent financial support. The authors thank Dr. Jayeeta Bhaumik of Center of Innovative and Applied Bioprocessing, Mohali for providing the kraft lignin derived from agricultural biomass.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apcata.2020.117730.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
